title: Clean Core & ABAP Cloud
description: Clean Core is SAP's platform engineering mandate for S/4HANA — keep the kernel stable, extend through well-defined APIs, and never modify SAP source. ABAP Cloud (Steampunk) is the runtime that enforces this. Together they represent a paradigm shift from modification-heavy custom ABAP to a structured, upgrade-safe extension architecture. For Staff+ engineers, this is not about compliance — it is about building a platform that scales across tenancy, cloud, and hybrid landscapes.

sections.title: Clean Core Philosophy

description.value:
Clean Core is the principle that the SAP system kernel (standard tables, programs, CDS views, classes delivered by SAP) must remain unmodified. All customer-specific requirements are implemented through extension mechanisms — BAdIs, CDS extension views, RAP business object extensions, side-by-side applications on BTP. This is not a cosmetic preference: it is a platform engineering decision that transforms the economics of SAP operations.

In the classic ABAP world, customers modified SAP standard objects directly — changing a BADI implementation, enhancing an MPE (bAdI-related) structure, or appending custom code to SAP programs. Each modification created a technical debt snowball: every SAP upgrade required regression testing, code reconciliation, and manual reapplication of modifications. A typical enterprise with 500+ custom modifications would spend 4–6 weeks on upgrade regression alone. Clean Core eliminates this by enforcing a strict layering boundary — SAP code is the immutable kernel, customer code is the extensible shell.

Staff+ Perspective: Clean Core is not an SAP constraint — it is an instance of the stable dependency principle (Robert C. Martin's principle that dependencies should point in the direction of stability). SAP delivers the stable kernel; customer extensions depend on SAP but SAP does not depend on customer extensions. This enables independent upgrade cycles — the same reasoning that drives microservice versioning and API contract management in distributed systems.

sections.codeBlock:
" Bad extensibility — modifying SAP standard directly
MODIFY zcustom_tab FROM ls_data.
" ... years later, SAP changes structure in EHP -> runtime dump

" Good extensibility — BAdI implementation
CLASS zcl_badi_salesprice IMPLEMENTATION.
  METHOD IF_EX_SALESPRICE~OVERRIDE_PRICE.
    " Runs at extension point, SAP code unchanged
    ev_price = iv_base_price * 1.05.
  ENDMETHOD.
ENDCLASS.

" Good extensibility — CDS extension view
extend view I_SalesOrder with Z_I_SO_EXT
{
  Z_CustomSurcharge
}

" Architecture rule: NO direct modification of SAP objects.
" All changes go through released extension points.

decisionTree.prompt: Should this requirement be implemented as a core modification, a BAdI, a CDS extension, or a side-by-side BTP application?
decisionTree.firstQuestion: "Does the requirement change SAP's standard business logic or data model structure?" If yes -> check if a released extension point (BAdI, CDS extend, RAP BO extension) is available. If no released point exists -> side-by-side BTP. If the requirement is purely UI/UX -> key user extensibility or adaptation.

branches.condition: Released BAdI exists for the business logic point
branches.path: Implement BAdI. Zero upgrade impact. Implementation switches activate/disable per scope. Use BAdI enhancement implementations to isolate customer logic.

branches.condition: Standard CDS view needs additional fields
branches.path: CDS extension view (EXTEND VIEW) + append structure. Fields survive upgrades. Activate custom fields via CFEND Fiori app. No SAP object modified.

branches.condition: No extension point exists; requirement is tightly coupled to SAP process
branches.path: Side-by-side on BTP. Build CAP application that subscribes to SAP events (Event Mesh) or reads via OData APIs. Zero stack impact. Independent lifecycle.

branches.condition: Customer insists on direct modification despite Clean Core mandate
branches.path: Escalate to architecture board. Quantify upgrade cost: every modification adds 0.5-2 days of upgrade regression. A system with 200+ modifications takes 8+ weeks to upgrade vs 1 week with Clean Core.

decisionTree.staffPlusTip: Clean Core is the stable dependency principle applied to SAP. When a customer asks 'can I just modify this one SAP include?' the answer is always no — but you must offer three alternatives: BAdI, CDS extension, or side-by-side. Never block without unblocking.

sections.title: ABAP Cloud Model (Steampunk)

description.value:
ABAP Cloud (formerly known as Steampunk) is the new ABAP runtime environment designed for cloud-native development. It runs ABAP in a containerized, multi-tenant environment on Cloud Foundry (or Kubernetes). The key architectural difference from classic ABAP is strict API governance: only released APIs can be used. Every ABAP statement, class, function module, and CDS annotation is classified as released (cloud-safe), released-deprecated (will be removed), or not released (unsupported in cloud).

The ABAP Cloud model enforces Clean Core by design: you cannot access unreleased SAP objects. The compiler itself blocks access to internal SAP classes, unreleased function modules, and direct table access. This is not a guideline — it is a compiler-enforced contract. If you try to CALL FUNCTION 'SOME_UNRELEASED_FM' in ABAP Cloud, the syntax check fails with a message like "Function module is not released for ABAP Cloud."

Design Principles: (1) API-first — all interfaces are explicit, released, and versioned. (2) Extension-by-contract — customers extend through explicit extension points (BAdIs, CDS extension views), not by patching SAP code. (3) Cloud-optimized runtime — the ABAP server is stateless, session management is delegated to Cloud Foundry, and persistence is managed by HANA. (4) Tenant isolation — each tenant gets an isolated ABAP session with its own data and metadata.

Staff+ Perspective: ABAP Cloud is SAP's response to the same forces that drove AWS Lambda (serverless), Google Cloud Run (containerized runtimes), and Heroku (platform-as-a-service). The ABAP language is treated as a first-class cloud runtime — not a legacy language to be migrated away from, but a strategic platform for enterprise cloud applications.

sections.codeBlock:
" ABAP Cloud class — only released APIs allowed
CLASS zcl_cloud_sales_processor DEFINITION
  PUBLIC FINAL CREATE PUBLIC.

  PUBLIC SECTION.
    METHODS process_sales_order
      IMPORTING iv_sales_order_id TYPE sales_order_id
      RAISING   cx_rap_business_exception.

  PROTECTED SECTION.
  PRIVATE SECTION.
    DATA mo_rap TYPE REF TO if_rap_entity.
ENDCLASS.

CLASS zcl_cloud_sales_processor IMPLEMENTATION.
  METHOD process_sales_order.
    " OK: released RAP interface view
    SELECT SINGLE *
      FROM I_SalesOrderTP
      WHERE SalesOrderID = @iv_sales_order_id
      INTO @DATA(ls_order).

    " COMPILE ERROR: not released for ABAP Cloud
    " CALL FUNCTION 'SOME_OLD_FM'.

    " OK: released BAdI interface
    DATA(lo_badi) = CAST if_salesorder_badi(
      cl_badi_factory=>get_instance( )->get( 'SALESORDER' ) ).
    lo_badi->override_price( iv_base_price = ls_order-TotalNetAmount ).
  ENDMETHOD.
ENDCLASS.

comparisonTable.title: Classic ABAP vs ABAP Cloud (Steampunk)
rows: Only released APIs via API radar. Compiler-enforced.
Direct modification of SAP objects (user exits, enhancements)
Extension points only (BAdIs, CDS extend, RAP BO extension)
Significant regression risk; modifications must be re-tested
Zero modification impact; extension points survive upgrades
Stateless runtime; session state in customer session context
Authorization via PFCG + authority checks in code
DCL row-level security + OAuth 2.0 + communication arrangements
GCTS / abapGit / add-on build pipeline in CI/CD
Only via released CDS views (I_ / C_ views); no direct table access
All ABAP types including dynpro, ALV, RFC
RAP + OData + CDS; no dynpro; no classical RFC; cloud-optimized types
Cloud-ready; passes SAP Cloud Readiness Check
comparisonTable.staffPlusInsight: The compiler-enforced API restriction is the most significant change. In classic ABAP, you can access anything — which creates maintenance nightmares. ABAP Cloud treats the compiler as the first line of governance. This is analogous to how Go enforces import path restrictions or how Java module system (JPMS) restricts access to internal JDK APIs.

sections.title: Stable API Layer & Released APIs

description.value:
The Stable API Layer is the contract between SAP and its customers. SAP guarantees that released APIs will remain stable across feature pack stacks (FPS) and major releases. This means once an API is marked as released (C1 or C2 in the API radar), you can depend on it without fear of breaking changes. The API radar is the central catalog: every released API is classified, documented, and stamped with a stability contract.

I_ (Interface) Views are the cornerstone of the stable API layer. These are CDS views specifically designed for external consumption — they abstract from the underlying table structure and provide a stable field contract. For example, I_SalesOrder is a released interface view that exposes sales order data. SAP can change the underlying tables in an upgrade, but as long as I_SalesOrder retains its field contract, your code continues to work without changes.

Staff+ Perspective: The Stable API Layer is SAP's version of API versioning and backward compatibility — the same patterns used by AWS (API Gateway versioning), Google Cloud (API deprecation policy), and Stripe (API version headers). The key architectural insight is that stability is a contract, not an implementation detail. SAP commits to maintaining the API contract across releases, and in exchange, customers commit to only using released APIs. This is the same bilateral contract that underpins the Linux kernel's stable API policy or the JDK's backward compatibility guarantee.

sections.codeBlock:
" Accessing cloud-ready released APIs

" Ok: released interface view I_SalesOrder
SELECT SalesOrderID, TotalNetAmount
  FROM I_SalesOrder
  WHERE SalesOrganization = '1000'
  INTO TABLE @DATA(lt_orders).

" Ok: released BAdI call
TRY.
    DATA(lo_badi) = cl_badi_factory=>get_instance( )->get(
      filter_name  = 'SALESORDER'
      instance     = lt_orders[ 1 ]-SalesOrderID
      context_name = 'MAINTAIN' ).
    lo_badi->if_salesorder_badi~modify_header(
      CHANGING cs_header = ls_header ).
  CATCH cx_badi_not_implemented.
    " BAdI not implemented — valid, proceed with default
ENDTRY.

" Error: accessing unreleased SAP internal class
" "CL_WB_OBJECT" -> compile error in ABAP Cloud
" DATA(lo_internal) = NEW cl_wb_object( ).

" Error: direct table access on unreleased table
" SELECT * FROM VBAP -> compile error
" Must use I_SalesOrderItem instead.

quantifiedTradeoff.title: Released API Stability vs Internal API Convenience

variables.name: API access rate
variables.source: Enterprise S/4HANA system with 200+ custom programs

variables.name: Breakage rate (internal APIs)
variables.value: ~12% of internal API usages break per upgrade
variables.source: SAP internal data on modification-based systems

released.monthlyPenalty: $0 — Released APIs never break. Zero upgrade-related rework. Initial adaptation cost: 1-2 days to switch from internal to released API.

internal.monthlyPenalty: 500 APIs × 12% × 3 days × 6 upgrades = 1,080 person-days/year (~4.3 FTE). Plus regression testing: 5 days per upgrade cycle. Total: ~1,200 person-days/year.

calculation.breakeven: Switching from internal to released APIs pays for itself after the first upgrade cycle. Even at 5% annual breakage rate, the cost of fixing 25 broken APIs (75 person-days) exceeds the upfront migration cost for the same APIs.

sections.title: Extension Points & Side-by-Side Extensibility

description.value:
Clean Core extensibility follows a tiered decision framework: (1) In-App Extensibility — if the requirement extends standard business logic (e.g., modify pricing, add a field to a purchase order), use in-app mechanisms: CDS BAdIs for logic extensions, CDS extension views for data model extensions, key user apps for UI/field extensions. These are deployed inside the S/4HANA tenant and are upgrade-safe because they never modify SAP objects. (2) Side-by-Side Extensibility — if the requirement involves new processes, external system integration, or loosely coupled functionality, build on BTP using CAP, Cloud Integration, or Event Mesh. Side-by-side extensions have their own lifecycle, runtime, and deployment pipeline.

The decision between in-app and side-by-side depends on coupling and lifecycle. If the extension is tightly coupled to SAP business objects (e.g., a custom validation on a sales order), in-app is appropriate because it must execute atomically with the SAP transaction. If the extension is an independent process (e.g., a web portal for customer order tracking), side-by-side is better because it allows independent scaling, deployment, and technology choice.

Staff+ Perspective: This tiered extensibility model mirrors the strangler fig pattern (Martin Fowler) for incremental system modernization. You start by extending in-app for tightly coupled concerns, then progressively migrate capabilities to side-by-side as the platform matures. The goal is not to maximize side-by-side — it is to minimize coupling. A clean core system with well-defined extension boundaries can evolve independently of SAP's release cadence.

sections.codeBlock:
" BAdI definition in Clean Core — customer extension point
" Enhancement Spot: ES_SALESORDER
" BAdI Definition: BADI_SALESORDER_PRICE

" BAdI Interface
INTERFACE if_salesorder_price_badi.
  METHODS override_price
    IMPORTING iv_base_price       TYPE netwr
              iv_currency          TYPE waerk
              iv_customer_group    TYPE kdgrp
    CHANGING  cv_final_price       TYPE netwr
    RAISING   cx_badi_abort.
ENDINTERFACE.

" Customer Implementation
CLASS zcl_badi_salesorder_customprice
  DEFINITION PUBLIC FINAL CREATE PUBLIC.
  PUBLIC SECTION.
    INTERFACES if_salesorder_price_badi.
ENDCLASS.

CLASS zcl_badi_salesorder_customprice IMPLEMENTATION.
  METHOD if_salesorder_price_badi~override_price.
    " Business logic: 5% surcharge for premium customers
    IF iv_customer_group = 'PREM'.
      cv_final_price = iv_base_price * 1.05.
    ELSE.
      cv_final_price = iv_base_price.
    ENDIF.
  ENDMETHOD.
ENDCLASS.

adversarialResponse.challenge: "Why build a BAdI for this? I can just enhance the standard SAP function module directly — it takes half the time."
adversarialResponse.badResponse: "Because SAP says not to modify standard objects." (dogmatic, no reasoning)
adversarialResponse.goodResponse: "I understand the short-term appeal — a direct enhancement takes 2 hours versus 6 hours to build a proper BAdI. But let me reframe the cost model. A direct modification creates a merge conflict on every single upgrade. With 2 major upgrades per year, each requiring 1 day to test and reapply the modification, that is 2 days per year of recurring cost. Over a 5-year system lifespan, the direct approach costs 2 hours + 10 days = ~82 hours. The BAdI costs 6 hours upfront + 0 recurring = 6 hours. The BAdI is cheaper by 14x over 5 years. Additionally, the BAdI is registered in the extension point catalog — visible to other developers, documented, and governable. The direct modification is invisible unless you grep the entire system. Clean Core is not about dogma; it is about making the total cost of ownership visible and minimizing it over the system lifecycle."
adversarialResponse.pattern: quantified-tradeoff — acknowledge the short-term time saving, quantify recurring upgrade cost, show cumulative 5-year TCO, and highlight hidden costs (discoverability, governance, measurability).

sections.title: Key User Extensibility

description.value:
Key User Extensibility empowers business analysts and power users to extend SAP applications without development skills. Through Fiori apps like Custom Fields (CFEND), Custom Logic (CLOG), and Custom Analytical Queries, business users can add fields, define simple validation rules, and create reports — all without touching ABAP code. This is a critical capability for Clean Core because it reduces the pressure on the IT team to build custom developments while keeping the system maintainable.

Custom Fields: The user creates a custom field in a business context (e.g., Sales — add a field "Sales Rep Commission" to the sales order). The system generates the required include structure, appends it to the database table, extends the corresponding CDS views, and makes the field available in Fiori UIs — all automatically. The custom field is stored in a customer namespace (prefix Z or Y) and survives upgrades because SAP never overwrites extension include structures.

Custom Logic: For business rules that need more than a new field (e.g., "if order amount > $10K, require VP approval"), the Custom Logic app provides a guided expression builder. The user selects trigger conditions and defines actions using a simplified rule syntax. Under the hood, the system generates a BAdI implementation or a RAP behavior extension — the user never sees ABAP, but the result is upgrade-safe Clean Core compliant code.

Staff+ Perspective: Key User Extensibility is SAP's answer to low-code / no-code platforms like Salesforce Lightning Platform, Microsoft Power Platform, and Mendix. The architectural insight is that platform capabilities should be layered: simple extensions should be possible without code; complex extensions should be possible with code — but all extensions must be upgrade-safe. This is the same layered approach used by Shopify (Liquid templates for merchants, custom apps for developers) and WordPress (plugins for users, custom development for engineers).

organizationalDimension.title: Key User Extensibility — Organizational Adoption Framework

stakeholders.incentive: Faster time-to-market for field changes without IT backlog. Can add fields in hours, not weeks.
stakeholders.risk: Uncontrolled proliferation of custom fields — each field adds complexity to the tenant metadata.
stakeholders.mitigation: Set a per-business-context limit (e.g., max 50 custom fields per context). Quarterly cleanup review.

stakeholders.incentive: Reduced custom development workload. Key user changes are transportable and documented automatically.
stakeholders.risk: Key users may create overlapping or conflicting custom fields across contexts leading to data inconsistency.
stakeholders.mitigation: Establish a naming convention (Z_<context>_<field>) enforced by a pre-activation check BAdI.

stakeholders.incentive: Clean Core compliance without developer bottleneck. Extensions are upgrade-safe by construction.
stakeholders.risk: Key user extensibility can be used to bypass proper architecture reviews for changes that belong in side-by-side BTP.
stakeholders.mitigation: Define a tiered extensibility policy: fields/logic inside the tenant go through key user extensibility; process/UI changes outside the tenant go through BTP. Publish a decision matrix accessible from the Fiori Launchpad.

organizationalDimension.staffPlusInsight: Key User Extensibility is not just a feature — it is an organizational change. It shifts ownership of simple extensions from IT to business, but requires governance to prevent chaos. The Staff+ architect must design the guardrails (limits, naming conventions, pre-activation checks) while empowering business users to move fast. Analogous to how platform engineering teams provide internal developer platforms with golden paths — constraint + autonomy.

sections.title: Clean Core Migration Strategy

description.value:
Migrating an existing S/4HANA system to Clean Core is a phased process. The goal is to eliminate all SAP object modifications while preserving business functionality. The two primary strategies are brownfield (clean the existing system in-place through refactoring) and greenfield (build a new clean system alongside, then switch over). The choice depends on the extent of modifications, business risk tolerance, and timeline.

Phase 1 — Assessment: Run the Clean Core Readiness Check (transaction /n/ATL/READY_CHECK or the Cloud Readiness Check tool). This produces a report of all custom code categorized by: (1) released API available — can be replaced, (2) extension point available — can be converted to BAdI, (3) no released API — must be migrated to side-by-side or escalated. Typical enterprises find 30-50% of their modifications fall into category 1 (easy wins), 30-40% category 2 (moderate effort), and 10-20% category 3 (significant investment).

Phase 2 — Refactoring: Replace each category (1) modification with its released API equivalent. Convert category (2) modifications to BAdIs or CDS extensions. For category (3), assess business value — many unreleased modifications can be eliminated entirely when the business realizes the process is now obsolete. For truly necessary modifications without released APIs, build side-by-side on BTP.

Staff+ Perspective: Clean Core migration is a technical debt reduction program — the same kind of initiative as migrating from Java 8 to Java 17 (removing deprecated API usage), upgrading from AngularJS to React (replacing patterns), or moving from a monolith to microservices (decomposing by bounded context). The key insight is that you cannot boil the ocean. Prioritize the 20% of modifications that cause 80% of upgrade pain. Use a strangler fig pattern: wrap old modifications in an adapter layer, then incrementally replace them.

sections.codeBlock:
" Clean Core Cleanup Report Pattern
" Run after each refactoring sprint to track progress

CLASS zcl_clean_core_report DEFINITION
  PUBLIC FINAL CREATE PUBLIC.

  PUBLIC SECTION.
    TYPES: BEGIN OF ty_modification_status,
             object_type    TYPE trobjtype,
             object_name    TYPE sobj_name,
             status         TYPE string, " CLEAN | IN_PROGRESS | BLOCKED
             released_api   TYPE string,
             owner          TYPE uname,
             sprint         TYPE i,
           END OF ty_modification_status.

    METHODS get_clean_core_score
      RETURNING VALUE(rv_score) TYPE i.  " 0-100%

    METHODS get_blockers
      RETURNING VALUE(rt_blockers) TYPE string_table.

  PRIVATE SECTION.
    DATA mt_modifications TYPE STANDARD TABLE OF ty_modification_status.
ENDCLASS.

CLASS zcl_clean_core_report IMPLEMENTATION.
  METHOD get_clean_core_score.
    DATA(lv_total) = lines( mt_modifications ).
    DATA(lv_clean) = REDUCE i( INIT x = 0
      FOR <m> IN mt_modifications WHERE ( status = 'CLEAN' )
      NEXT x = x + 1 ).
    IF lv_total > 0.
      rv_score = lv_clean * 100 / lv_total.
    ELSE.
      rv_score = 100.
    ENDIF.
  ENDMETHOD.

  METHOD get_blockers.
    LOOP AT mt_modifications INTO DATA(ls_mod)
      WHERE status = 'BLOCKED'.
      APPEND |{ ls_mod-object_type } { ls_mod-object_name }|
        TO rt_blockers.
    ENDLOOP.
  ENDMETHOD.
ENDCLASS.

quantifiedTradeoff.title: Brownfield vs Greenfield Clean Core Migration

variables.name: Custom modification count
variables.source: Enterprise ECC → S/4HANA brownfield with ~20 years of custom code

variables.name: Modification categories
variables.source: Typical distribution from Cloud Readiness Check reports

brownfield.monthlyPenalty: $380K one-time. Risk: production regression across 450 changes. Rollback plan required per sprint. Downtime: 0 (zero-downtime refactoring via BAdI switches).

greenfield.writeLatency: 2,100 person-days (new clean tenant + re-implementation of business processes)
greenfield.monthlyPenalty: $840K one-time. Risk: lower (no production regression, parallel run). Downtime: 1 weekend for cutover. Additional platform cost: BTP consumption + new tenant.

calculation.breakeven: Greenfield is 2.2x more expensive upfront but carries 60% lower risk of production incident. Choose brownfield when business cannot tolerate long migration timeline (<12 months). Choose greenfield when the existing system has deep architectural debt (modifications in core processes like Order-to-Cash) that would make brownfield refactoring equally risky.

sections.title: Staff+ Clean Core Governance

description.value:
At the Staff+ level, Clean Core is not a technical problem — it is an organizational adoption problem. The technology is straightforward (use released APIs, build extension points, avoid modifications). The hard part is changing organizational behavior: developers who have modified SAP objects for 15+ years, business process owners who expect workarounds, and project managers who optimize for immediate delivery over long-term maintainability.

Governance Framework: (1) Policy — a written Clean Core policy document signed by the CIO and SAP CoE lead. Defines what constitutes a violation, the escalation path, and exception process. (2) Metrics — a Clean Core Score (0-100%) that measures the percentage of custom objects that comply with Clean Core principles. Tracked quarterly with a target trajectory. (3) Guardrails — automated enforcement via ATC (ABAP Test Cocktail) variants that check for unreleased API usage. Transport request checks that block transports containing modifications to SAP objects. (4) CI/CD Integration — the ATC check is integrated into the CI/CD pipeline (e.g., SAP Cloud Transport Management, Jenkins, GitHub Actions). Any modification of an SAP object causes the pipeline to fail.

Staff+ Role: The Staff+ architect must (1) design the governance framework, (2) build the automated enforcement tooling, (3) negotiate exceptions with the architecture board, and (4) mentor developers on Clean Core patterns. The governance framework must be pragmatic — allow exceptions with a clear justification and remediation timeline, but make violations visible at the executive level.

sections.codeBlock:
" Clean Core Compliance Check Program
" Run daily / weekly to flag violations

CLASS zcl_clean_core_compliance DEFINITION
  PUBLIC FINAL CREATE PUBLIC.

  PUBLIC SECTION.
    INTERFACES if_amdp_marker_hdb.

    TYPES: BEGIN OF ty_violation,
             object_type   TYPE trobjtype,
             object_name   TYPE sobj_name,
             violation_type TYPE string,
             released_api  TYPE string,
             created_by    TYPE uname,
             created_date  TYPE dats,
           END OF ty_violation.

    METHODS check_all_objects
      RETURNING VALUE(rt_violations) TYPE STANDARD TABLE OF ty_violation.

    METHODS generate_scorecard
      RETURNING VALUE(rv_score) TYPE i.

  PRIVATE SECTION.
    METHODS check_sap_modifications
      RETURNING VALUE(rt_mods) TYPE STANDARD TABLE OF ty_violation.

    METHODS check_unreleased_apis
      RETURNING VALUE(rt_apis) TYPE STANDARD TABLE OF ty_violation.
ENDCLASS.

CLASS zcl_clean_core_compliance IMPLEMENTATION.
  METHOD check_all_objects.
    " 1. Check for modifications to SAP standard objects
    "    (table mods, program mods, CDS view mods, etc.)
    rt_violations = CORRESPONDING #(
      check_sap_modifications( ) ).

    " 2. Check for unreleased API usage
    "    (function modules, classes not marked as released)
    APPEND LINES OF check_unreleased_apis( ) TO rt_violations.

    " 3. Check for direct table access to SAP tables
    "    (SELECT from VBAP, BKPF, etc.)
    "    ... additional checks ...
  ENDMETHOD.

  METHOD check_sap_modifications.
    SELECT obj_type, obj_name, author, moddate
      FROM reposrv
      WHERE obj_name LIKE 'Z%'
        AND obj_type NOT LIKE 'Z%'
      INTO TABLE @DATA(lt_mods).

    LOOP AT lt_mods ASSIGNING FIELD-SYMBOL(<mod>).
      APPEND INITIAL LINE TO rt_mods ASSIGNING FIELD-SYMBOL(<v>).
      <v>-object_type = <mod>-obj_type.
      <v>-object_name = <mod>-obj_name.
      <v>-violation_type = 'MODIFICATION'.
      <v>-created_by = <mod>-author.
      <v>-created_date = <mod>-moddate.
    ENDLOOP.
  ENDMETHOD.

  METHOD generate_scorecard.
    DATA(lt_all) = check_all_objects( ).
    DATA(lv_total) = 100.  " Total custom objects from repository
    DATA(lv_compliant) = lv_total - lines( lt_all ).
    IF lv_total > 0.
      rv_score = lv_compliant * 100 / lv_total.
    ELSE.
      rv_score = 100.
    ENDIF.
  ENDMETHOD.
ENDCLASS.

" Scheduled via: /nSM36 -> job Z_CLEAN_CORE_AUDIT

adversarialResponse.challenge: "All this Clean Core governance is slowing us down. We used to deliver a change in 2 days; now it takes 2 weeks because we have to go through the ATC checks, architecture review, and exception process."
adversarialResponse.badResponse: "Clean Core is non-negotiable. The process stays." (inflexible, ignores velocity concern)
adversarialResponse.goodResponse: "I understand the frustration — the governance overhead is real, especially for small changes. Let me address this with three concrete improvements. First, I will set up an 'express lane' for changes that are clearly Clean Core compliant — if the ATC check passes automatically (no SAP object touches) and the change uses only released APIs, the change is auto-approved with no architecture review. That should cover ~70% of changes. Second, I will introduce an ATC quick-check that runs in <10 seconds instead of the full 2-minute scan, so developers get feedback before they even create a transport. Third, for the remaining 30% of changes that touch an SAP object or use a non-released API, I will establish a weekly architecture sync — a 30-minute slot where developers present their exception request and get an immediate decision. The goal is to make compliance fast for the common case and predictable for the exceptional case. Governance should accelerate, not decelerate — if it's slowing us down, we fix the governance, not the compliance."
adversarialResponse.pattern: empathy-and-system-design-optimization — acknowledge friction, categorize changes to apply appropriate process, design express lanes for low-risk changes, introduce fast feedback loops, and reframe governance as an enabler not a gate.

details: Staff+ Insight: Clean Core and ABAP Cloud represent SAP's pivot from a modification-based platform to an extensibility-based platform. The architectural principles parallel those of modern cloud platforms: stable APIs, extension-by-contract, compiler-enforced governance, and upgrade-safe customization. Interviewers probing Clean Core want to see that you grasp the platform engineering perspective — not just compliance rules, but the economic and architectural reasoning behind them.
